faq:
Frequently Asked Questions

If motion-sensor lights are not very effective, why install them at all?

They work best as an early warning system rather than a primary defense, since they may briefly startle an animal and alert you to activity. Just be sure they complement physical exclusion rather than replace it.

Why do raccoons stop reacting to the lights so quickly?

Predators habituate to lights, sounds, and other non-lethal deterrents within days to weeks once they learn there is no real danger. In Santa Cruz County, raccoons that are already used to residential lighting adjust especially fast.

What actually keeps nighttime predators out of a coop?

Physical exclusion is the only reliable defense: half-inch hardware cloth over every opening, two-step latches, and buried wire aprons or solid floors to stop digging. A covered run also helps against climbing and aerial predators.

Frequently Asked Questions

How big does the container need to be for a potted blackberry?

Use a pot of at least 15 to 20 gallons with good drainage holes, and fill it with quality potting mix rather than garden soil, which compacts in containers. The larger volume holds moisture longer and gives the roots room to establish.

Will blackberries still produce in foggy coastal Santa Cruz?

Yes. Blackberries tolerate cooler, foggier conditions better than many fruiting plants, so they remain viable across the region. In foggier spots, placing the pot against a south-facing wall helps capture extra warmth and light.

How do I prune a potted blackberry through the year?

Remove canes once they have fruited, since they will not produce again, and keep the new canes for next season. Canes go dormant over winter, then resume growth in spring.

Frequently Asked Questions

Is crop rotation worth it if I only have two or three raised beds?

Yes. Even a simple rotation helps prevent soil-borne diseases and reduces pest buildup, and any rotation beats none. If space is tight, just alternating nightshades between two beds every other year still makes a difference.

Which plant groups should I rotate between?

A workable pattern uses four groups: nightshades (tomatoes, peppers, eggplant), legumes (beans, peas), brassicas (broccoli, kale, cabbage), and root crops or alliums (carrots, beets, garlic, onions). Move each group to the next bed from season to season.

Why does rotation matter more in a mild climate like Santa Cruz?

Local winters rarely get cold enough to kill soil pathogens naturally, so diseases and root-knot nematodes can accumulate over time without rotation. Rotating out of nightshades for at least two years significantly reduces fusarium wilt and other soil-borne problems.

Frequently Asked Questions

How can I tell which predator attacked my flock?

Read the evidence left behind, since each predator leaves a pattern. A bird pulled partly through wire or eaten at the head points to a raccoon, multiple birds killed with puncture wounds at the skull suggests a weasel, and an entire bird missing with only feathers left points to a fox.

Is regular chicken wire enough to keep predators out?

No. Standard chicken wire keeps chickens in but does not keep predators out. Use half-inch hardware cloth on every opening instead.

What should I check after a predator visit?

Inspect the coop after dark for any gap larger than one inch, since small predators like ringtail cats and weasels squeeze through tiny openings. Also look for digging signs and add a buried wire apron or solid floor along with two-step latches.

Frequently Asked Questions

Is January a good time to plant fruit trees in Santa Cruz County?

Yes. January is the prime window for bare-root fruit trees while they are still dormant, before growth breaks in spring. Good local choices include Santa Rosa plums, Blenheim apricots, Babcock peaches, and Fuji apples.

Should I spray my fruit trees after winter pruning?

Apply a dormant spray of horticultural oil and copper fungicide on a dry day above 40 degrees to help prevent fungal disease. Doing this after pruning, while trees are leafless, gives the best coverage.

What pests should I watch for in a cool, wet January?

Snails and slugs thrive in cool, damp conditions, so hand-pick them or use iron phosphate baits. Also keep an eye on drainage, since waterlogged soil can lead to root rot.

Frequently Asked Questions

What can I direct-sow outdoors in January?

Cold-hardy crops do well now, including peas, fava beans, radishes, spinach, and lettuce. Soaking pea seeds overnight before planting helps speed up germination.

Does my location in the county change what I should plant?

Yes. Frost-prone areas like Bonny Doon and the upper San Lorenzo Valley should stick to hardy greens and root vegetables, while milder coastal zones can also set out nursery starts of broccoli, cabbage, and kale.

Did I miss the window if I never planted garlic in fall?

Not entirely. Garlic and onion sets can still go in during January if you missed autumn planting. It is also a good time to amend beds with compost or aged manure before spring.

Frequently Asked Questions

When is the best time to prune deciduous fruit trees in Santa Cruz?

Prune during dormancy, generally late January through mid-February for most varieties, after the harshest weather but before new growth begins. Stone fruit like peaches and plums can be done in late January, while apples and pears are better in February.

Are apricots pruned at the same time as other fruit trees?

No. Apricots are the exception and should be pruned in late summer after harvest to prevent Eutypa dieback, a fungal disease. Pruning them in the wet dormant season raises the risk of infection.

How should I prepare my tools before pruning?

Sharpen your pruning tools and disinfect them with a 10 percent bleach solution or isopropyl alcohol. Clean, sharp cuts heal faster and reduce the spread of disease, which matters in our foggy climate.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can lettuce really grow outdoors through a Santa Cruz winter?

Yes. Lettuce and most salad greens grow best between 45 and 75 degrees, which matches the region's mild winter temperatures. Winter greens also tend to taste sweeter, bolt more slowly, and face fewer pests than summer crops.

Do I need to cover my greens when it gets cold?

It depends on your location. In the San Lorenzo Valley and other frost-prone areas, use lightweight frost cloth when temperatures drop below 28 degrees, while coastal gardens can usually grow uncovered. Hardy greens like kale, collards, and chard tolerate frost without cover and taste sweeter after cold.

How do I keep a steady supply of salad greens?

Sow a small batch of seeds every two to three weeks for a continuous harvest rather than one large crop. Press seeds lightly into the surface since they need light to germinate, and use cut-and-come-again harvesting to extend the season.

Frequently Asked Questions

Is the California coast actually frost-free?

Not entirely. Areas right along the coast from Santa Cruz to Capitola rarely dip below 32 degrees, but inland spots like Scotts Valley and the San Lorenzo Valley regularly see the mid-20s between December and February. Knowing your local low temperatures tells you whether protection is needed.

Should I use plastic sheeting to cover my plants?

No. Use frost cloth, or row cover, rather than plastic, because plastic conducts cold directly to the leaves and can make damage worse. Drape the cloth over a frame so it does not touch the foliage, and secure the edges.

Does watering before a frost help?

Yes. Water the garden the afternoon before an expected frost, since moist soil holds heat better than dry soil and releases it slowly overnight. Cover vulnerable plants like citrus, lettuce, and seedlings before sunset.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why do my hens stop laying in winter?

Shorter days are usually the cause. Hens need about 14 hours of light per day to sustain regular laying, and Santa Cruz drops to roughly 9.5 hours in December, which triggers a natural pause. This rest is normal and healthy.

Could my hens' diet be reducing egg production?

Yes. Hens need 16 to 18 percent protein plus steady access to calcium, and too many scratch grains or kitchen scraps can crowd out their layer feed. Keep layer feed as the main diet and offer free-choice oyster shell.

My hen looks healthy but I am finding no eggs. What else could it be?

Free-ranging hens sometimes lay in hidden spots outside the nesting boxes, so search secluded corners of the yard. Molting, stress, and age can also pause or reduce laying.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the single best amendment for heavy clay soil?

Compost, added generously and regularly. Worked into the top 6 to 8 inches, compost creates air pockets between the clay particles, improving both drainage and root penetration. Clay is actually fertile once its structure improves.

Should I add sand to break up my clay?

No, not on its own. UC ANR advises against adding sand without plenty of organic matter, because the combination can set up like concrete and end up worse than the original soil. Stick with compost instead.

When should I avoid working clay soil?

Never work clay while it is wet. Use the squeeze test: if the soil forms a sticky ball, wait several dry days, since working wet clay damages its structure and creates hard clumps that take months to break down.

Frequently Asked Questions

What exactly are chill hours?

Chill hours are the total hours between 32 and 45 degrees that accumulate during winter dormancy. Fruit trees need a certain number to break dormancy and fruit well, and too few can mean delayed growth, weak flowering, and little fruit.

How many chill hours does Santa Cruz County get?

Roughly 400 to 800 hours, depending on location. Coastal areas from Santa Cruz to Aptos average about 400 to 500, while inland zones like Scotts Valley and the San Lorenzo Valley receive 600 to 800, with Watsonville in between.

Which fruit trees suit a low-chill coastal garden?

Low-chill varieties are the safer bet near the coast, such as Anna or Dorsett Golden apples, Santa Rosa plum, and Improved Dwarf fig. Inland gardeners with more chill can consider mid-chill options like Fuji apples and Blenheim apricots.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why do ducks stop laying in winter?

The main reason is reduced daylight. Ducks need roughly 14 to 16 hours of light per day to maintain consistent laying, and coastal areas like Santa Cruz drop to about 9.5 hours in December. Even heavy layers like Khaki Campbells and Runners slow down or stop.

Is it normal for my duck to stop laying for months?

Yes, it is a healthy seasonal pause. Many ducks stop by mid-November and resume in late January or early February, using the rest to maintain feathers and rebuild reserves.

Besides daylight, what else can reduce laying?

Age plays a role, as ducks over three to four years naturally lay less, and gaps in calcium and protein can lower output, so offer a 16 to 18 percent protein layer feed plus free-choice oyster shell. Stress from predators and hidden outdoor nests can also be factors.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why do my ducks splash so much water out of their containers?

Splashing is instinctive, not a bad habit. Dabbling, dunking, and washing are hardwired behaviors ducks use to eat, clean their bills, flush their nostrils, and groom their feathers.

Is there a waterer that splashes less?

Use a narrow, deep container, around 6 inches wide and 8 inches deep, that lets a duck submerge its head but not its whole body. A 5-gallon bucket with a nipple waterer also works, though ducks prefer open water for bathing.

How do I keep the duck area from turning to mud?

Set water stations on gravel or wood-chip pads with good drainage, and keep them away from the coop to avoid wet bedding, which can lead to respiratory issues and bumblefoot. Draining the bathing water onto garden beds every few days puts it to good use.

Frequently Asked Questions

Do backyard ducks really need a pond?

No formal pond is needed. What ducks must have is water deep enough to fully submerge their heads, which keeps their eyes, nostrils, and mucous membranes clean and free of infection. They do not need to swim to stay healthy, though they clearly enjoy it.

What can I use instead of a pond?

Simple options work well, such as a rubber livestock bucket, a concrete mixing tub, or a deep dish for basic head-dunking, plus a kiddie pool or deep tub for bathing. Make sure there are ramps or sloped edges for safe entry and exit.

How do I keep duck water clean?

Drain and refill regularly rather than letting water go stagnant, since ducks eat, mess, and track mud through it quickly. In warm weather stagnant water can attract mosquitoes, and the soiled water makes good nutrient-rich irrigation for garden beds.

Frequently Asked Questions

How big can my shed be before I need a building permit?

Both the City and unincorporated County exempt detached storage sheds under 120 square feet from building permits, as long as they meet other conditions like a single story and a height no more than 10 feet. Keep in mind that no building permit does not mean no rules.

Does adding power or water to my shed change anything?

Yes. Adding electrical wiring, plumbing, heating, or cooling triggers separate permits regardless of the shed's size exemption. A bare storage shed is treated very differently from one set up as a workspace.

Do setback rules still apply to an exempt shed?

Yes. Zoning setbacks always apply, and they vary by jurisdiction and zone. In the unincorporated County a shed may sit as close as 3 feet from side or rear property lines, while the City requires at least 6 feet from other structures, so confirm your specific zone in writing first.
